sourceOfTruth: services/runner/src/permission-plan.ts (verified line-by-line; docs are narrative summaries of this code)
malformedFallbackNotes: Two distinct malformed cases both fall back to {default:"ask", rules:[]}, never toward running a tool: (1) request.permissions is present but is not a plain object (e.g. a string or array); (2) request.permissions.default is present but is not one of the four valid modes. If request.permissions is absent entirely, that is NOT malformed -- it uses the normal default policy allow_reads.

killSwitch.effect: permissionsFromRequest short-circuits to {default: "deny", rules: []} before the wire permissions block is even read.
killSwitch.scopeNuance: This is an operator kill-switch on the PLAN, not on the per-gate ladder. effectivePermission() still checks gate.specPermission and gate.serverPermission BEFORE consulting plan.default. So a tool with an explicit authored permission of "allow" still runs even with the kill-switch on (see testVectors TV13/TV14). Docs describe this informally as forcing deny "everywhere"; the code is narrower. This is a documented ambiguity resolved in favor of the code per project convention (ground-truth wins).

precedence.description: The resolved tool spec's own explicit permission (allow/ask/deny), if the author set one. Always wins, in both directions: an explicit allow runs even under a deny policy; an explicit ask/deny binds even under an allow policy.
If the tool belongs to a user-declared MCP server, that server's own explicit permission, when the tool itself carries none.

An authored builtin rule (runner.permissions.rules) whose pattern matches the gate's tool name, optionally with a Claude-settings-style prefix on the first string argument (e.g. "Bash(rm:*)"). If multiple rules match, the highest-severity verdict wins: deny > ask > allow. A prefix rule whose args cannot be inspected (no string argument found) never matches; it falls through to the default rather than guessing.

The run's policy mode (permissions.default). Under allow_reads, the tool's read-only hint decides: true -> allow, anything else (false or absent) -> ask. Under allow/ask/deny, the mode applies directly regardless of the read-only hint.

verdictLadder.description: Refuse the call. No event beyond the ordinary tool_call/tool_result-as-error.
Run the call in place. No extra event.

Look for a decision already recorded in this conversation, keyed by the stable anchor approvedCallKey(toolName, canonicalizedArgs). Found and it is "allow" or "deny": apply it and consume it (a stored decision is used exactly once). Not found: pendingApproval -- pause the turn (wire stopReason: "paused"), emit exactly one interaction_request(user_approval) event, and wait for a human on the next turn.

gates.label: Claude .claude/settings.json (static, pre-answered)
gates.description: Rendered once before the session starts by the SDK's claude_settings.py. Every backend-resolved executable tool gets a per-tool allow/deny rule addressed as mcp__agenta-tools__<name>. A tool whose effective permission is allow gets an allow rule (Claude runs it with no gate); deny gets a deny rule; ask or unset gets no rule, so Claude's own gate still fires. This is Gate 1; without it an explicit allow would still pause, because Claude's gate fires before the ACP responder or the relay ever sees the call.

gates.label: ACP Approval Responder (dynamic)
gates.description: Answers a permission gate Claude raises itself over session.onPermissionRequest, for any call the settings.json layer left undecided (ask/unset). Calls the same decide() as the relay. This is Gate 2.

gates.label: Tool Relay enforcement
gates.description: Enforces decide() directly for every tool Pi's relay executes: gateway/code/reference/platform callback tools, and (via the pi-builtin-gating extension hook) Pi's own native builtins. Pi has no separate harness-side settings gate, so the relay is the single enforcement point for everything Pi runs. Claude's gateway/code calls also funnel through the same dispatch function, but RelayPermissions.enforce is false for Claude because Gate 1 and Gate 2 already decided before the call reaches the relay.

gates.label: Client-tool ladder (onClientTool)
gates.description: A carve-out, not the policy-and-rules ladder above. There is no local "allow" outcome for a client tool: an explicit deny, or a deny policy default with no explicit permission, blocks the call; every other case pauses as pendingApproval, forwarded to the browser to fulfill on a later turn (the tool's only job is to reach the browser, so it never resolves locally). An explicit permission always wins in both directions; when it resolves to "ask", a stored deny decision recorded earlier in the conversation can still deny it, the same as the policy ladder. A stored browser output (from a prior turn) fulfills the call directly without asking again; the client-tool output store is a FIFO list per key, separate from the allow/deny decision store, so a client output whose literal value is the string "allow"/"deny" can never be misread as a permission decision.

toolExamples.name: read
toolExamples.notes: Pi builtin; ruleName "Read" in the Claude-settings-style rule vocabulary.

toolExamples.name: bash
toolExamples.notes: Pi builtin; ruleName "Bash". Not read-only, so allow_reads asks by default. Rule args are canonicalized to just {command} for stable resume matching.

toolExamples.name: github__create_issue
toolExamples.notes: Resolved to a callback spec with call_ref tools.composio.github.create_issue.<connection>. No read-only hint set by the author normally means it asks under allow_reads unless the tool config sets an explicit permission.

toolExamples.name: query_spans
toolExamples.notes: Platform op with read_only: true in the catalog; auto-allows under allow_reads.

toolExamples.name: commit_revision
toolExamples.notes: Mutating platform op ("update yourself"); asks under allow_reads unless explicitly allowed.

toolExamples.name: fx
toolExamples.notes: Inline code tool; no catalog-derived read-only hint, so an unset hint under allow_reads asks (absent counts as a write).

toolExamples.name: request_connection
toolExamples.notes: Client tool; decided by the client-tool ladder, not the policy/rule ladder. There is no local allow: an explicit deny (or a deny policy default with no explicit permission) blocks the call, and every other case always pauses as pendingApproval, forwarded to the browser to fulfill.

toolExamples.name: github (user MCP server tool)
toolExamples.notes: User-declared MCP servers reach Claude only (gated by AGENTA_AGENT_ENABLE_MCP, off by default) and are dropped for Pi even when the flag is on. The owning server's explicit permission (serverPermission) is consulted before the rule/default ladder.

testVectors.decidedBy: policyDefault(allow_reads): not read-only -> ask -> no stored decision
ruleMatch: Bash(rm:*) -> ask -> no stored decision
ruleMatch: Bash(rm:*) -> deny (overrides the allow policy default)
specPermission=deny (explicit tool permission always wins, step 1)
specPermission=allow overrides the deny policy default (explicit tool permission wins in both directions)
serverPermission=ask (step 2, checked before rule/default; owning MCP server's explicit permission) -> no stored decision
policyDefault(allow): mode applies directly, read-only hint ignored
policyDefault(deny): mode applies directly even to a read-only tool
malformedFallback: permissions is present but not a record -> {default:"ask"} -> no stored decision
malformedFallback: default is not one of allow/ask/deny/allow_reads -> {default:"ask"} -> no stored decision

config.storedDecision: allow (from a prior turn's approval, keyed by approvedCallKey("Bash", {command:"ls -la"}))

testVectors.decidedBy: storedDecision=allow, consumed once (ask permission resolved from a replayed approval)
killSwitch: plan forced to {default:"deny", rules:[]} before the request's permissions block is read; no explicit spec/server permission on this gate, so the forced default applies
specPermission=allow still wins even with the kill-switch active, because effectivePermission checks step 1 before consulting the (kill-switch-forced) plan default -- see killSwitch.scopeNuance
